bins.name: State versus federal jurisdiction over retail interconnections
bins.desc: SCE argues FERC lacks historical jurisdiction over retail load interconnections in California, which are regulated by the CPUC. A federal rule on cost allocation risks intruding on state authority to set retail service terms. Any rule must be narrowly tailored to avoid jurisdictional challenges that could delay implementation.
quotes: In California, the Public Utilities Commission ("CPUC") regulates the interconnection of retail (e.g., end-use) customers to SCE's system and FERC has not historically exercised authority in this area.

Any Commission rule that intrudes on the states' authority to determine the terms and conditions of retail service—even retail service to large data center loads that arguably affect wholesale rates—would be problematic from a jurisdictional perspective.

the ANOPR's eighth reform principle, which suggests a new federal standard for how transmission costs should be allocated to large load retail customers, risks directly intruding on retail ratemaking in jurisdictions that have retained bundled retail service.
any rule must be carefully crafted to avoid, to the extent possible, any future jurisdictional challenges that will unnecessarily delay implementation.

the Commission should recognize and support the need for this type of proactive planning while ensuring that any future Commission rule does not conflict with existing state reliability planning processes, and should give deference to the established processes governed by the state regulators.

bins.name: Cost allocation for large load interconnection
bins.desc: SCE supports requiring large load customers to bear their own interconnection and transmission use costs. However, existing utility customers must be protected from these costs; durable loads may eventually roll costs into rates via credits or refunds if they persist. Protections must prevent existing customers from absorbing stranded or underutilized assets.
quotes: it is critical to balance speed to interconnection with utility customer protections by ensuring that large load customers are responsible for the costs to facilitate their interconnection to the transmission system and their use of the transmission system.
existing customers should not bear the interconnection costs for new large load customers, but that those costs may ultimately be rolled into rates (through credits/refunds) if the load is durable.
Protections should also be in place to ensure utility customers are not left carrying the costs of stranded or underutilized assets built to interconnect large loads.

bins.name: Resource adequacy and reliability planning before interconnection
bins.desc: SCE emphasizes proactive reliability studies and integrated resource planning are critical before large loads interconnect, to ensure sufficient generation capacity. Federal rules should defer to California's established state planning processes (Resource Adequacy, Integrated Resource Planning, and CAISO Transmission Planning) rather than conflicting with them.
quotes: Given the potential size of large data centers seeking to interconnect, it is critical that proactive reliability studies and integrated planning are thoroughly executed to ensure there is sufficient generation capacity to serve large loads upon interconnection.

the Commission should recognize and support the need for this type of proactive planning while ensuring that any future Commission rule does not conflict with existing state reliability planning processes, and should give deference to the established processes governed by the state regulators.

bins.name: NOPR with draft procedures before final rule
bins.desc: SCE opposes jumping directly to a final rule from high-level ANOPR principles. The Commission should follow the precedent of Order 2003, issuing a full Notice of Proposed Rulemaking with draft pro forma procedures and agreements, allowing stakeholders to comment on actual language through working groups and technical conferences.
quotes: SCE urges the Commission to first issue a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking ("NOPR") rather than proceeding to a final rule based solely on the high-level principles outlined in the ANOPR and the necessarily high-level comments provided in response.
A NOPR should include actual draft procedures and agreements, which would allow stakeholders to provide more specific input.

publishing a full proposed rulemaking that includes a pro forma agreement and procedures and allowing full comment on firm proposals, including through working groups and technical conferences, will allow more robust input from all stakeholders.
